aircraft\\italy.json:
[
  {
    "type": "Biplane Fighter",
    "year": 1939,
    "engine": "Fiat A.74 RC.38, 840 hp",
    "armament": "2× 12.7mm Breda-SAFAT machine guns",
    "range_km": 775,
    "max_speed_kmh": 441,
    "id": "fiat-cr-42-falco",
    "name": "Fiat CR.42 Falco",
    "nation": "Italy",
    "summary": "The last major biplane fighter to see combat, the CR.42 was Italy's frontline fighter at war's start. Outclassed by monoplane opponents, it transitioned to ground-attack and night harassment roles."
  },
  {
    "crew": 1,
    "role": "Air superiority fighter",
    "type": "Fighter",
    "engine": "Alfa Romeo RA.1000 RC.41 (Daimler-Benz DB 601A licence), 1,175 hp",
    "armament": [
      "2× 12.7mm Breda-SAFAT machine guns",
      "2× 7.7mm machine guns"
    ],
    "range_km": 765,
    "manufacturer": "Macchi",
    "max_speed_kmh": 595,
    "units_produced": 1220,
    "years_of_service": "1941–1945",
    "id": "mc-202-folgore",
    "name": "Macchi C.202 Folgore",
    "nation": "Italy",
    "summary": "The finest Italian fighter of the war. Armed with a German Daimler-Benz DB 601 engine (as used in the Bf 109E), the Folgore was surprisingly competitive with contemporary Allied fighters, though Italian production constraints limited its numbers. It fought effectively in North Africa and the Mediterranean."
  },
  {
    "crew": 5,
    "role": "Maritime strike / bomber",
    "type": "Torpedo Bomber / Medium Bomber",
    "armament": [
      "3× 12.7mm machine guns",
      "2× 450mm torpedoes or 1,250kg bombs"
    ],
    "range_km": 1900,
    "manufacturer": "Savoia-Marchetti",
    "max_speed_kmh": 430,
    "units_produced": 1217,
    "years_of_service": "1936–1945",
    "id": "sm-79-sparviero",
    "name": "Savoia-Marchetti SM.79 Sparviero",
    "nation": "Italy",
    "summary": "The SM.79, known as the 'Hunchback' for its distinctive dorsal hump, was the most successful Italian aircraft of the war in its torpedo bomber role. Italian torpedo bomber pilots — the Aerosiluranti — achieved remarkable results against Allied shipping in the Mediterranean, sinking several warships including cruisers."
  }
]

[
  {
    "type": "Army Fighter",
    "year": 1942,
    "engine": "Kawasaki Ha-40 (Daimler-Benz DB 601 licence), 1,175 hp",

    "name": "Kawasaki Ki-61 Hien",
    "nation": "Japan",

  },
  {
    "crew": 1,
    "role": "Carrier-based fighter",
    "type": "Fighter",
    "engine": "Nakajima NK1C Sakae 12, 940 hp",
    "armament": [
      "2× 20mm Type 99 cannon",
      "2× 7.7mm Type 97 machine guns"
    ],
    "range_km": 3105,
    "ceiling_m": 10000,
    "manufacturer": "Mitsubishi",
    "max_speed_kmh": 534,
    "units_produced": 10939,
    "years_of_service": "1940–1945",
    "id": "a6m-zero",
    "name": "Mitsubishi A6M Zero",
    "nation": "Japan",
    "summary": "The A6M Zero was the finest carrier-based fighter in the world at the time of its introduction and came as a complete shock to Allied pilots in 1941. Its extraordinary range and manoeuvrability — achieved by sacrificing pilot armour and self-sealing fuel tanks — made it initially dominant. By 1943, improved Allied fighters and tactics had overcome its advantages, and its lack of protection made it increasingly deadly to its own pilots.",
    "image": "/storage/images/armaments/aircraft/a6m-zero.jpg"
  },
  {
    "crew": 1,
    "role": "Army air superiority fighter",
    "type": "Fighter",
    "engine": "Nakajima Ha-115, 1,150 hp",
    "armament": [
      "2× 12.7mm Ho-103 machine guns"
    ],
    "range_km": 1760,
    "manufacturer": "Nakajima",
    "max_speed_kmh": 530,
    "units_produced": 5919,
    "years_of_service": "1941–1945",
    "id": "ki-43-hayabusa",
    "name": "Nakajima Ki-43 Hayabusa",
    "nation": "Japan",
    "summary": "The Imperial Japanese Army Air Force's primary fighter, known to Allied pilots as 'Oscar'. Like the Zero, it prioritised manoeuvrability over protection. The most numerous Japanese army fighter of the war, it served on every front from China to Burma and the Pacific.",
    "image": "/storage/images/armaments/aircraft/ki43.jpg"
  },
  {
    "crew": 1,
    "role": "Multi-role fighter",
    "type": "Fighter",
    "armament": [
      "2× 20mm Ho-5 cannon",
      "2× 12.7mm Ho-103 machine guns"
    ],
    "range_km": 2168,
    "manufacturer": "Nakajima",
    "max_speed_kmh": 631,
    "units_produced": 3514,
    "years_of_service": "1944–1945",
    "id": "ki-84-hayate",
    "name": "Nakajima Ki-84 Hayate",
    "nation": "Japan",
    "summary": "Considered the best Japanese fighter of the war. The Ki-84 Hayate ('Gale') could match or outperform the P-51D Mustang at lower altitudes and represented Japan's last serious attempt to produce a competitive front-line fighter. Material shortages and poor-quality fuel limited its operational effectiveness in the war's final year.",
    "image": "/storage/images/armaments/aircraft/ki84.jpg"
  }
]

missiles\\germany.json:
[
  {
    "id": "v1",
    "name": "V-1 Flying Bomb (Fieseler Fi 103)",
    "type": "Cruise Missile",
    "nation": "Germany",
    "years_of_service": "1944–1945",
    "warhead_kg": 850,
    "range_km": 250,
    "speed_kmh": 640,
    "guidance": "Gyroscope + pre-set range counter",
    "total_launched": 10492,
    "summary": "The world's first operational cruise missile. Powered by a pulsejet engine producing the distinctive buzzing sound, the V-1 was the first of the Vergeltungswaffe programme. Primarily targeting London and Antwerp, it killed approximately 22,892 people. Allied countermeasures — fast fighters, barrage balloons, radar-directed AA guns — destroyed over 4,000 before they reached their targets."
  },
  {
    "id": "v2",
    "name": "V-2 Rocket (Aggregat A-4)",
    "type": "Ballistic Missile",
    "nation": "Germany",
    "years_of_service": "1944–1945",
    "warhead_kg": 910,
    "range_km": 320,
    "max_speed_kmh": 5760,
    "guidance": "Inertial (gyroscope)",
    "total_launched": 3172,
    "summary": "The world's first long-range guided ballistic missile and the first man-made object to reach space (crossing the Kármán line during test flights). Developed at Peenemünde under Wernher von Braun, its supersonic speed made interception impossible. It killed approximately 9,000 civilians, but more forced labourers died building it. Its technology became the foundation of both American and Soviet space programmes."
  },
  {
    "id": "hs-293",
    "name": "Henschel Hs 293",
    "type": "Anti-ship Guided Missile",
    "nation": "Germany",
    "years_of_service": "1943–1944",
    "warhead_kg": 295,
    "range_km": 18,
    "guidance": "Radio command (joystick)",
    "summary": "The world's first operational anti-ship guided missile. A glide bomb fitted with a rocket motor, the Hs 293 was directed to its target by a radio operator in the launching aircraft. It sank or damaged numerous Allied warships in the Mediterranean and Atlantic, including the sloops HMS Egret and HMCS Athabaskan, before Allied jamming and tactical adaptations reduced its effectiveness."
  }
]
missiles\\japan.json:
{
  "category": "Guided Weapons & Missiles — Japan",
  "weapons": [
    {
      "name": "Ohka Model 11",
      "type": "Piloted Flying Bomb (Kamikaze)",
      "year": 1944,
      "length_m": 6.07,
      "wingspan_m": 5.12,
      "warhead_kg": 1200,
      "max_speed_kmh": 740,
      "notes": "The Ohka ('Cherry Blossom') was a rocket-powered piloted flying bomb, carried under a Mitsubishi G4M bomber and released near the target. Allied sailors called it 'Baka' (fool). Sank USS Mannert L. Abele in April 1945."
    },
    {
      "name": "Type 93 Long Lance Torpedo",
      "type": "Surface-Launched Torpedo",
      "year": 1933,
      "length_m": 9.0,
      "diameter_mm": 610,
      "warhead_kg": 490,
      "range_km": 40,
      "speed_knots": 48,
      "notes": "The finest torpedo of the war. Oxygen-fuelled (leaving no bubble trail), it had four times the range of contemporary US torpedoes. Gave Japanese destroyers a decisive night-combat advantage in the Guadalcanal campaign."
    },
    {
      "name": "Kaiten Type 1",
      "type": "Human-Guided Torpedo",
      "year": 1944,
      "length_m": 14.75,
      "warhead_kg": 1550,
      "speed_knots": 30,
      "notes": "A manned suicide torpedo, essentially a modified Type 93 with a cockpit welded on. The pilot could not escape after launch. Deployed from submarines late 1944–1945; sank USS Mississinewa."
    }
  ]
}

    "name": "Battleship Yamato",
    "nation": "Japan",
    "summary": "The Yamato was the largest and most powerfully armed battleship ever built, carrying the largest guns ever fitted to a warship. Yet she was largely ineffective in combat — the era of the battleship had given way to the aircraft carrier. She was sunk by American carrier aircraft during Operation Ten-Go on 7 April 1945, on a suicide mission to Okinawa with fuel for only a one-way trip."
  },

    "name": "Fleet Carrier Shōkaku",
    "nation": "Japan",
    "summary": "One of Japan's finest fleet carriers and veteran of Pearl Harbor, the Coral Sea, and Santa Cruz. Shōkaku and her sister Zuikaku were the premier carrier strike force of the Imperial Japanese Navy. She was sunk by US submarine Cavalla during the Battle of the Philippine Sea ('The Marianas Turkey Shoot')."
  },

  }
]

wunderwaffen\\germany.json:
[
  {
    "id": "v1-flying-bomb",
    "name": "Fieseler Fi 103 / V-1 Flying Bomb",
    "designation": "Vergeltungswaffe 1 (V-1)",
    "type": "Cruise Missile",
    "nation": "Germany",
    "image": "/storage/images/armaments/wunderwaffen/v1.jpg",
    "years_of_service": "1944–1945",
    "manufacturer": "Fieseler",
    "warhead_kg": 850,
    "range_km": 250,
    "speed_kmh": 640,
    "total_launched": 10492,
    "summary": "The V-1 was the world's first cruise missile and the first of Hitler's Vergeltungswaffe ('Vengeance Weapons'). Powered by a pulsejet engine, its distinctive buzzing sound earned it the British nickname 'Doodlebug' or 'Buzz Bomb'. Launched primarily against London and Antwerp from June 1944, it killed approximately 22,892 people. Allied air defences — including barrage balloons, anti-aircraft guns, and fast fighters — eventually neutralised much of the threat."
  },
  {
    "id": "v2-rocket",
    "name": "Aggregat 4 / V-2 Rocket",
    "designation": "Vergeltungswaffe 2 (V-2)",
    "type": "Ballistic Missile",
    "nation": "Germany",

    "years_of_service": "1944–1945",

    "principal_designer": "Wernher von Braun",
    "warhead_kg": 910,
    "range_km": 320,
    "max_speed_kmh": 5760,
    "total_launched": 3172,
    "summary": "The V-2 was the world's first long-range guided ballistic missile and the first man-made object to reach space. Developed at Peenemünde under Wernher von Braun, it travelled at supersonic speeds, making it impossible to intercept with any technology of the era. Approximately 20,000 forced labourers died producing the rockets — more than the 9,000 civilians killed by V-2 strikes. Its technology directly influenced both the American and Soviet postwar space programmes."
  },
  {
    "id": "me-163-komet",
    "name": "Messerschmitt Me 163 Komet",
    "type": "Rocket-powered interceptor",
    "nation": "Germany",
    "image": "/storage/images/armaments/wunderwaffen/me163.jpg",
    "years_of_service": "1944–1945",
    "manufacturer": "Messerschmitt AG",
    "crew": 1,
    "max_speed_kmh": 960,
    "range_km": 80,
    "endurance_minutes": 8,
    "armament": ["2× 30mm MK 108 cannon"],
    "units_produced": 370,
    "summary": "The Me 163 Komet was the only rocket-powered aircraft to fly combat missions in history. Its maximum speed of nearly 960 km/h made it the fastest aircraft of the war — so fast that Allied pilots reported being unable to track it. However, its powered endurance of only 8 minutes, extreme handling characteristics, and the explosive instability of its C-Stoff/T-Stoff fuel (which ignited on contact with organic material including pilots) made it as dangerous to its crew as to the enemy."
  },
  {
    "id": "schwerer-gustav",
    "name": "Schwerer Gustav / Dora",
    "type": "Railway Gun",
    "nation": "Germany",
    "image": "/storage/images/armaments/wunderwaffen/schwerer-gustav.jpg",
    "years_of_service": "1942–1945",
    "manufacturer": "Krupp",
    "crew": 2500,
    "caliber_mm": 800,
    "shell_weight_kg": 7100,
    "range_km": 47,
    "units_produced": 2,
    "summary": "Schwerer Gustav was the largest calibre gun ever used in combat and fired the heaviest shells in artillery history. Each shell weighed 7,100 kg and could penetrate 7 metres of concrete. It was used during the Siege of Sevastopol in 1942, firing 48 rounds into Soviet fortifications. The gun required a crew of 2,500 men and special twin railway tracks — its tactical impact was negligible compared to the resources consumed in its construction and deployment.",

    "range_km": 165,
    "summary": "The Hochdruckpumpe (High Pressure Pump), designated V-3, was a supergun concept with 130-metre barrels angled toward London, built into a bunker at Mimoyecques, France. Lateral propellant charges along the barrel were meant to accelerate the shell progressively. RAF bombing raids, including bouncing bomb attacks by 617 Squadron, rendered the installation unusable before it could fire a shot. Two shorter versions were used against Luxembourg and Luxemburg, firing 183 rounds.",

{
  "category": "Naval Vessels — Other Axis Powers",
  "vessels": [
    {

      "armament": "9× 460mm guns (largest naval guns ever fitted to a warship), 12× 155mm guns, 24× 127mm AA guns",

    },
    {
      "name": "Shōkaku",
      "nation": "Japan",

      "class": "Shōkaku class",
      "commissioned": 1941,

      "aircraft": 72,

      "nation": "Japan",
